label: Fire Drill
descriptive_clues: A fire drill is practice for getting out of the building safely. | A bell rings loudly and everyone walks outside in a line. | Try it like this: 'It's just a fire drill.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for the school team: take a photo of your fire-drill rally point — the spot outside where your class gathers. A predictable visual makes the drill enormously less scary. Pair the tile with the language you use ("we go to our spot").

label: Lockdown Drill
descriptive_clues: A lockdown drill is practice for staying safe and quiet inside. | The class moves away from the door and keeps very still. | Try it like this: 'It's just a drill, we're safe.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for the school team: take a photo of the calm corner of your classroom where students gather during a lockdown drill. Keep the image friendly and unambiguous — this is about predictability, not alarm.

label: Field Trip
descriptive_clues: A field trip is when your class travels somewhere fun to learn. | You ride a bus to a place like a zoo or a museum. | Try it like this: 'We have a field trip!'
prompt_template: SKELETON for the school team: snap a photo of the school bus or your class lining up at the door for a field trip. Use the same photo across trips; what changes is the destination tile that follows.

label: Library Day
descriptive_clues: Library day is when your class visits the library. | You pick out a book to borrow and bring home to read. | Try it like this: 'Today is library day.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for the school team: photo of your school library entrance, or the librarian, or the rug where storytime happens. Schedule it as a recurring tile on library days.

label: Picture Day
descriptive_clues: Picture day is when someone takes your photo at school. | You smile for the camera, and the photo goes home to your family. | Try it like this: 'It's picture day!'
prompt_template: SKELETON for the school team: photo of the photo-backdrop area, or a friendly camera icon. Surface this only on picture day so the student knows what to expect.

label: Show and Tell
descriptive_clues: Show and tell is when you bring something special to share. | You hold it up and tell the class all about it. | Try it like this: 'I have show and tell.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for the school team: photo of the rug, podium, or "presenter spot" your class uses. Pair with a sentence-starter strip if the student needs scaffolding.

label: PE
descriptive_clues: PE is the special class where you move and play games. | You run, stretch, and play with balls in the gym. | Try it like this: 'We have PE today.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for the school team: photo of the gym door or PE teacher. PE is loud and physical — a predictable visual helps an AAC user transition into it.

label: Art
descriptive_clues: Art is the special class where you make things and create. | You paint, draw, cut, and glue to make something of your own. | Try it like this: 'I love art class.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for the school team: photo of the art room or art teacher. Pair with material tiles (paint, clay, glue) the student can request.

label: Music
descriptive_clues: Music is the special class where you sing and play sounds. | You sing songs, clap rhythms, and play instruments. | Try it like this: 'We have music today.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for the school team: photo of the music room, music teacher, or a familiar instrument. Music is often a strong reinforcer for AAC learners — make it requestable.

label: Lunchroom
descriptive_clues: The lunchroom is the big room where everyone eats lunch. | You sit at long tables with kids from your school. | Try it like this: 'We go to the lunchroom.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for the school team: photo of your cafeteria (or the table the student's class sits at). Use the same lunchroom image, then layer the menu choices as separate tiles.

label: Arrival
descriptive_clues: Arrival is the start of the school day, when you get there. | You hang up your backpack and say good morning. | Try it like this: 'I just got to school.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for the school team: photo of the classroom entrance from the hallway side, OR the drop-off door. Helps the student name the start of the school day.

label: Dismissal
descriptive_clues: Dismissal is the end of the school day, when you go home. | You grab your backpack and head to the bus or car. | Try it like this: 'It's time to go home.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for the school team: photo of the dismissal door, OR the bus loop, OR the line-up spot. Pair with "Bye" gestalt for an end-of-day script.

label: Centers
descriptive_clues: Centers are different play and learning spots around the room. | You move from one to the next — blocks, books, art, and more. | Try it like this: 'It's center time.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for the school team: photo of your centers rotation chart, or the rug/table where centers happen. If you can, make one tile per center (reading center, math center, etc.) and link them all to this one.

label: Line up
descriptive_clues: Lining up means standing one behind the other in a row. | You wait in line so the class can walk somewhere together. | Try it like this: 'Time to line up.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for the school team: photo of the line-up spot at the door, ideally with the painted line or floor marker visible. Pair with "follow" or "go" gestalts.

label: Quiet time
descriptive_clues: Quiet time is a calm part of the day with very little noise. | You rest, look at a book, or take a little break. | Try it like this: 'It's quiet time now.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for the school team: photo of the cozy corner, rest mat, or whatever signals quiet time in your room. Pair with a "calm" tile for self-regulation language.

label: Calm corner
descriptive_clues: The calm corner is a cozy spot to go when you need to settle. | You can take deep breaths and feel better there. | Try it like this: 'I need the calm corner.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for the school team: photo of the calm-down spot in your room (beanbag, sensory bin, etc.). Make the tile available as a request so the student can ask to go there.

label: My teacher
descriptive_clues: Your teacher is the grown-up who helps your class learn. | They read with you, keep you safe, and cheer you on. | Try it like this: 'That's my teacher.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for the school team: head-and-shoulders photo of the lead teacher. Each year this tile updates — keep the slug, swap the image.

label: My aide
descriptive_clues: Your aide is a grown-up who helps you through the day. | They stay close by to help with work and tricky moments. | Try it like this: 'My aide helps me.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for the school team: head-and-shoulders photo of the paraprofessional/aide working with the student. Replace per year / per assignment.

label: Class pet
descriptive_clues: A class pet is an animal that lives in your classroom. | The whole class takes turns feeding it and caring for it. | Try it like this: 'I want to see the class pet.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for the school team: photo of your class pet (or class mascot stuffie). Strong daily-vocabulary anchor.

label: My class job
descriptive_clues: A class job is a little task that is yours to do. | You might be the line leader or hand out the papers. | Try it like this: 'I have a class job.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for the school team: photo of the chart spot showing this student's class job (line leader, paper passer, etc.). Update weekly with the rotation.

label: Therapy room
descriptive_clues: The therapy room is a special room where you work with your helper. | It has toys and tools that make practicing fun. | Try it like this: 'We go to the therapy room.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for the therapist: photo of YOUR therapy room from the doorway. The single most useful transition cue for an AAC user.

label: Session start
descriptive_clues: Session start is the beginning of your time with your helper. | You say hello and get ready to play and practice. | Try it like this: 'We're starting now.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for the therapist: photo of your session-start routine (greeting board, name card, whatever you open with). Bracket the session in visuals so {reference} knows what to expect.

label: Session end
descriptive_clues: Session end is when your time with your helper is finished. | You clean up, say goodbye, and head out. | Try it like this: 'All done with therapy.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for the therapist: photo of your end-of-session marker (timer hitting zero, "all done" card, the door). Pair with the "all done" gestalt.

label: My reinforcer
descriptive_clues: A reinforcer is a fun reward you get for trying hard. | After good work, you earn a turn with something you love. | Try it like this: 'I want my reward.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for the therapist: photo of THIS child's strongest reinforcer (the bubble machine, their iPad video, the spinner). Make it requestable; reinforce by handing over what they asked for.

label: Sensory bin
descriptive_clues: A sensory bin is a tub full of stuff to dig your hands into. | It might have beans, rice, or sand to scoop and squish. | Try it like this: 'I want the sensory bin.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for the therapist: photo of the sensory bin {reference} engages with. If you swap the contents weekly, photo each variant as a separate tile (rice bin, bean bin, water bin) and link them.

label: Visual schedule
descriptive_clues: A visual schedule is a row of pictures that shows what comes next. | You look at it to know the plan for your time. | Try it like this: 'What's next on my schedule?'
prompt_template: SKELETON for the therapist: photo of your printed/laminated visual schedule. Surface this tile when transitioning between activities — "let's check the schedule".

label: Target word
descriptive_clues: A target word is the special word you're practicing right now. | You say it again and again to make it stronger. | Try it like this: 'I said my word!'
prompt_template: SKELETON for the therapist: this is a placeholder you duplicate per session target — photo of the actual referent you're working on (a specific toy, a specific snack). Update the label per target.

label: Co-treat partner
descriptive_clues: A co-treat partner is another helper who joins your session. | Two helpers work with you together at the same time. | Try it like this: 'We have two helpers today.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for the therapist: head-and-shoulders photo of the OT/SLP/BCBA you co-treat with. Helps the child anchor multi-person sessions.

label: Quiet space
descriptive_clues: A quiet space is a calm, soft spot to settle your body. | You can go there to breathe and feel calm when things are too much. | Try it like this: 'I need the quiet space.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for the therapist: photo of the calm corner / sensory tent / whatever de-escalation space you use. Available as a request — children CAN ask for a break.

label: Favorite toy here
descriptive_clues: This is the toy you love best in the therapy room. | You get to play with it as a treat for your hard work. | Try it like this: 'I want my favorite toy.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for the therapist: photo of the toy in YOUR room {reference} reliably reaches for. Different from a home favorite — this is what works in YOUR space.

label: Bedtime
descriptive_clues: Bedtime is when the day ends and you go to sleep. | You put on pajamas, brush your teeth, and snuggle in. | Try it like this: 'It's bedtime' or 'One more book, please.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for parents: photo of your bedtime routine's anchor moment (bath, brush teeth, books in the rocker). Pair with the "good night" gestalt for a complete bedtime script.

label: Grandma's house
descriptive_clues: Grandma's house is the place where your grandma lives. | You visit to see her, get hugs, and feel cozy. | Try it like this: 'We're going to Grandma's house.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for parents: photo of grandma's front door, OR grandma herself, OR the room your child plays in there. Helps {reference} name where they're going.

label: Grandpa's house
descriptive_clues: Grandpa's house is the place where your grandpa lives. | You visit to see him, play, and feel cozy. | Try it like this: 'We're going to Grandpa's house.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for parents: photo of grandpa's house OR grandpa himself. Make one tile per grandparent {reference} sees regularly.

label: My lovey
descriptive_clues: Your lovey is the special soft thing you snuggle for comfort. | It might be a blanket or a stuffed animal you love best. | Try it like this: 'I want my lovey.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for parents: photo of THE comfort object (the specific blanket, the specific stuffie). Use the child's actual word for it as the label.

label: Our pet
descriptive_clues: Your pet is the animal that lives with your family. | You feed it, pet it, and love it like part of the family. | Try it like this: 'I want to see our pet.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for parents: photo of YOUR family pet (not a stock dog/cat). Use the pet's real name as the label.

label: Our car
descriptive_clues: Your car is the vehicle your family rides in to go places. | You buckle up in your seat and ride along. | Try it like this: 'Let's get in the car.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for parents: photo of YOUR car (interior with the child's car seat visible works great). Pair with "go" + a destination tile.

label: Our park
descriptive_clues: This is the park your family likes to go to. | You know the swings and slides there because you visit a lot. | Try it like this: 'I want to go to our park.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for parents: photo of the playground or park you visit most. Adds a "where" answer {reference} can actually request.

label: Special snack
descriptive_clues: Your special snack is a treat you really love to eat. | It's the one you ask for and look forward to most. | Try it like this: 'I want my special snack.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for parents: photo of THE snack — the one you keep on the top shelf, the one that's the reinforcer. Specific is far better than generic.

label: Bath time
descriptive_clues: Bath time is when you get clean in the warm water. | You splash, play with bubbles, and wash up. | Try it like this: 'It's bath time!'
prompt_template: SKELETON for parents: photo of your bathtub with the bath toys / bubbles your child knows. Pair with "more" and "all done" for full bath-time language.

label: Favorite book
descriptive_clues: Your favorite book is the story you love to read again and again. | You know the pictures and want it read at bedtime. | Try it like this: 'Read my favorite book.'
prompt_template: SKELETON for parents: photo of the cover of THE book — the one you've read a hundred times. Pair with "read" or "again".

label: My show
descriptive_clues: Your show is the TV program you love to watch. | You know the characters and ask to see it. | Try it like this: 'I want to watch my show.'
prompt_template: SKELETON: photo (or screenshot) of the show your child reaches for. We do not bundle specific titles here for licensing reasons — every family fills this in with their own.

label: My movie
descriptive_clues: Your movie is the film you love to watch over and over. | You know all the parts and wait for your favorite scene. | Try it like this: 'I want my movie.'
prompt_template: SKELETON: photo (or poster) of the movie {reference} requests over and over. Same idea as "my show" but for a specific movie.

label: My song
descriptive_clues: Your song is the music you love to hear and move to. | You light up and want to dance when it comes on. | Try it like this: 'Play my song!'
prompt_template: SKELETON: image of the album cover, the speaker, or even just a friendly music note for THE song. Pair with "more" + "again" for full requesting language.

label: My character
descriptive_clues: Your character is the one from a show or book you love most. | You look for them in toys, books, and shows. | Try it like this: 'That's my character!'
prompt_template: SKELETON: image of the character (toy figure photo works great). The character your child names everything after.

label: My app
descriptive_clues: Your app is the game or program you love on a screen. | You tap and play it on a tablet or phone. | Try it like this: 'I want my app.'
prompt_template: SKELETON: photo of the iPad showing the app icon, or the icon itself. Specific app, not "an app" — the one that gets asked for.

label: am
descriptive_clues: 'Am' goes with 'I' to tell what you are doing or feeling. | You say it right after 'I'. | Try it like this: 'I am happy' or 'I am playing.'
prompt_template: A {style} of {reference} pointing to themselves with one thumb, simple equals sign next to them indicating "I am ___", one clear friendly figure centered on a plain soft pastel background; soft even lighting, bright cheerful colors, the meaning obvious at a glance to a toddler; minimal extra detail.

label: are
descriptive_clues: 'Are' goes with 'you' and 'we' to say what is happening right now. | 'We are playing' means the playing is happening right now. | You can say 'you are funny' or 'we are here!'
prompt_template: A {style} of {reference} and another young child side by side, each pointing at the other, a soft equals sign between them indicating "you/we are ___", one clear friendly figure centered on a plain soft pastel background; soft even lighting, bright cheerful colors, the meaning obvious at a glance to a toddler; minimal extra detail.

label: was
descriptive_clues: 'Was' is for something that already happened — it is finished now. | 'It was fun' means the fun already happened, before now. | You can say 'it was yummy' or 'I was sleeping.'
prompt_template: A {style} of {reference} looking back over their shoulder with a soft backward arrow indicating past tense, one clear friendly figure centered on a plain soft pastel background; soft even lighting, bright cheerful colors, the meaning obvious at a glance to a toddler; minimal extra detail.

label: have
descriptive_clues: 'Have' means it is yours, or it is in your hands right now. | 'I have a ball' means the ball is yours to hold. | You can say 'I have it!' or 'Can I have one?'
prompt_template: A {style} of {reference} holding a single small cheerful object with both hands at chest height, "I have ___", one clear friendly figure centered on a plain soft pastel background; soft even lighting, bright cheerful colors, the meaning obvious at a glance to a toddler; minimal extra detail.

label: has
descriptive_clues: 'Has' means someone else is holding it, or it is theirs. | 'She has the ball' means the ball is in her hands. | You can say 'he has it' or 'Mom has my cup.'
prompt_template: A {style} of {reference} gesturing toward a teddy bear next to another child, "she/he has ___", one clear friendly figure centered on a plain soft pastel background; soft even lighting, bright cheerful colors, the meaning obvious at a glance to a toddler; minimal extra detail.

label: had
descriptive_clues: 'Had' means you held it before, but that is finished now. | 'I had a cookie' means the cookie is already gone. | You can say 'I had one' or 'we had fun.'
prompt_template: A {style} of {reference} looking thoughtfully at an empty hand, with a soft past-tense backward arrow above, one clear friendly figure centered on a plain soft pastel background; soft even lighting, bright cheerful colors, the meaning obvious at a glance to a toddler; minimal extra detail.

label: could
descriptive_clues: 'Could' is a kind, gentle way to ask for something. | 'Could I have one?' asks nicely. 'Could you help?' asks for help kindly. | You can say 'could I have a turn?'
prompt_template: A {style} of {reference} shrugging gently with both palms up, a soft "maybe" curved arrow above them, one clear friendly figure centered on a plain soft pastel background; soft even lighting, bright cheerful colors, the meaning obvious at a glance to a toddler; minimal extra detail.

label: would
descriptive_clues: 'Would' is a polite way to ask, or to say what you'd like. | 'Would you help me?' asks kindly. 'I would like that' means yes, please. | You can say 'I would like more, please.'
prompt_template: A {style} of {reference} considering two small cheerful objects in front of them, finger tapping chin, one clear friendly figure centered on a plain soft pastel background; soft even lighting, bright cheerful colors, the meaning obvious at a glance to a toddler; minimal extra detail.

label: or
descriptive_clues: 'Or' shows a choice between two things. | You say it when you can pick one. | Try it like this: 'milk or water?' or 'this or that.'
prompt_template: A {style} of two cheerful objects (a small ball and a small star) on either side of a soft forked path icon, one clear friendly figure centered on a plain soft pastel background; soft even lighting, bright cheerful colors, the meaning obvious at a glance to a toddler; minimal extra detail.

label: so
descriptive_clues: 'So' tells what happened next because of it. | I was tired, so I slept. It broke, so we fixed it. | You can say 'I was hungry, so I ate.'
prompt_template: A {style} of a soft arrow leading from a small cheerful event icon to {reference} reacting with a small "got it!" face, one clear friendly figure centered on a plain soft pastel background; soft even lighting, bright cheerful colors, the meaning obvious at a glance to a toddler; minimal extra detail.

label: if
descriptive_clues: 'If' means maybe — it only happens when another thing happens first. | If it rains, we stay inside. If you're hungry, we eat. | You can say 'if you want' or 'what if?'
prompt_template: A {style} of a small cheerful question mark hovering above a forked path, gentle "what if?" feeling, one clear friendly figure centered on a plain soft pastel background; soft even lighting, bright cheerful colors, the meaning obvious at a glance to a toddler; minimal extra detail.

label: not
descriptive_clues: 'Not' is how you say something is wrong, or isn't what you want. | 'Not this one' means a different one. 'Not yet' means wait a little. | You can say 'not this one' or 'I'm not done.'
prompt_template: A {style} of {reference} gently shaking their head with one finger raised and a soft red diagonal "no" line near a small cheerful object, one clear friendly figure centered on a plain soft pastel background; soft even lighting, bright cheerful colors, the meaning obvious at a glance to a toddler; minimal extra detail.

label: don't
descriptive_clues: 'Don't' is how you tell someone to stop, or not to do a thing. | 'Don't touch' means hands off. 'Don't go' means stay with me. | You can say 'don't!' or 'please don't go.'
prompt_template: A {style} of {reference} holding up both flat hands in a polite refusing gesture, soft red diagonal line nearby, one clear friendly figure centered on a plain soft pastel background; soft even lighting, bright cheerful colors, the meaning obvious at a glance to a toddler; minimal extra detail.

label: at
descriptive_clues: 'At' points to a place or a spot. | You say it to tell where someone or something is. | Try it like this: 'I'm at home' or 'Look at me.'
prompt_template: A {style} of {reference} standing at a clearly marked spot (a small painted X on the floor), pointing down to it, one clear friendly figure centered on a plain soft pastel background; soft even lighting, bright cheerful colors, the meaning obvious at a glance to a toddler; minimal extra detail.

label: from
descriptive_clues: 'From' tells where something started or where it came. | You say it about where a thing or a person comes. | Try it like this: 'It's from Grandma' or 'I came from school.'
prompt_template: A {style} of a soft arrow leaving a small cheerful house and heading outward, indicating origin, one clear friendly figure centered on a plain soft pastel background; soft even lighting, bright cheerful colors, the meaning obvious at a glance to a toddler; minimal extra detail.

label: of
descriptive_clues: 'Of' tells what is inside, or what something is a piece from. | 'A cup of milk' is a cup with milk inside. 'A piece of cake' is one piece from the whole cake. | You can say 'a cup of milk' or 'a piece of cake.'
prompt_template: A {style} of a cheerful gift box being gently unwrapped by {reference}, revealing a small toy inside ("part of"), one clear friendly figure centered on a plain soft pastel background; soft even lighting, bright cheerful colors, the meaning obvious at a glance to a toddler; minimal extra detail.

label: through
descriptive_clues: 'Through' means going in one side and out the other. | You say it when you pass all the way across or inside something. | Try it like this: 'Go through the door' or 'Look through the window.'
prompt_template: A {style} of {reference} stepping through a small open doorway, soft motion lines, one clear friendly figure centered on a plain soft pastel background; soft even lighting, bright cheerful colors, the meaning obvious at a glance to a toddler; minimal extra detail.
